steps.m: A letter used to begin this chapter
steps.n: This is one of the special letters that begin some chapters of the Quran. While its exact meaning is known only to Allah, some scholars suggest it may be related to the word صدق (truth/honesty) or that it represents the letter ص itself.

steps.m: A letter used to begin this chapter
steps.n: The long vowel mark (ٓ) shows how this letter should be pronounced - as 'Saad' rather than just 's'
Basic past tense form of the verb
Changes from 'to read' into 'that which is read' - a special form used for divine scripture
Adding ال makes it refer specifically to THE divine book of Islam

steps.m: and The Quran (connected to صٓ before it)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect it to the mysterious letters (صٓ) that come before it in the verse

steps.m: By The Quran! (as an oath connected to ذِى ٱلذِّكْرِ after it)
steps.n: The kasra ending shows this is an oath, swearing by the Quran. It connects to 'possessor of the reminder' (ذِى ٱلذِّكْرِ) that follows it.
Added kasra (ِ) to connect it with what comes after it. This word shows that something possesses or has a quality of what follows it.
Added ى at the end to show this word is describing or connected to what comes after it (ٱلذِّكْرِ - the remembrance).
The root becomes a noun meaning 'remembrance' or 'mention'
Added vowels to make it a noun meaning the act or result of remembering
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ذ because it comes after ال. This word is connected to 'the Quran' in this ayah.
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to the word before it (ذِى)
This is a basic particle that introduces a contrasting idea or correction to what came before
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because it connects to the next word ٱلَّذِينَ (those who). This connection shows we're about to learn more details about what comes after 'but rather'
Added ن and َ at the end to make it refer to multiple people
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it mean 'those who'. This word connects to كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) by describing who did the action.

steps.m: to reject, to cover up truth, to be ungrateful

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is about something that happened in the past. This word describes what الَّذِينَ (those who) did.
Adding وا changes it from one person (he) to many people (they) doing the action. This matches with الَّذِينَ (those who) which refers to multiple people.
The small circle (۟) is just a pronunciation mark showing the وا is part of the word
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. In this ayah, it shows the disbelievers are in a state of pride.
The ي is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Root letters joined together with a shadda (double letter) to show 'he became' mighty
Changed the first vowel to kasra to make it a noun
Added ة to show one specific instance of might/pride

steps.m: in pride/might (describing the state of كَفَرُوا/those who disbelieved)
steps.n: Added two dots under (kasratan) to show 'in a state of pride' - tells us how the disbelievers were acting

steps.m: to split apart or oppose something

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun showing conflict between two groups
Added a mark showing this is affected by the word فِى (in) that comes before it
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
This is a question word that asks about quantity. In this ayah, it's asking about how many generations were destroyed.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Added hamza with fatha at start and sukun on second letter to make the causative form
Adds first person plural suffix to show the subject 'we'. This is a past tense verb, not a noun, so it does not take case endings.
This is a preposition showing where something starts. In this ayah, it connects 'We destroyed' (أَهْلَكْنَا) with 'before them' (قَبْلِهِم), showing when the destruction happened.

steps.m: to be in front of or before

steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning 'before'
Added kasra at the end because this word is being possessed by what comes after it (them)

steps.m: before them (referring to the previous generations mentioned in قَرْنٍ)
steps.n: Added هِم to show this means 'before them' - referring back to the previous generations (قَرْنٍ) mentioned in the ayah
Basic preposition that shows where something starts or what it's made of
Added shadda (doubled م) to make the word stronger. This happens because it comes after a pause in speech.
Added fatha and sukun to make it into a noun meaning a group of people joined together in time
Added tanween kasra to show this is an indefinite noun being acted upon by أَهْلَكْنَا (we destroyed)
Added vowels and changed final ي to ى to show past tense
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after something else
This is a special negative particle that usually comes before words about time
Adding the vowel mark helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: and there is not (specifically for time)
steps.n: Added 'wa' (and) at the start to connect with what came before, showing the timing of their regret

steps.m: and there is not (with extra emphasis)
steps.n: Doubles the 'wa' at the start to add extra emphasis to the negation
The kasra (ِ) vowel mark helps make this a noun meaning 'time'
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this word is being used as a direct object

steps.m: to run away or escape from something
a place or time to escape to
steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning and ا in the middle to create a noun meaning 'place/time of escape'

steps.m: any place/time of escape (in this ayah: when there was no longer any escape)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is indefinite (any escape). In this ayah, it comes after لَاتَ حِينَ (not a time of) to say there was no longer any possibility of escape.
Added vowels to show past tense feeling or emotion
Added وا to show multiple people were amazed

steps.m: and they were amazed (at أَن جَآءَهُم)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect to what comes before. The long alif and small و at the end are just writing styles that don't change the meaning. This word shows what they were amazed at - that a warner came to them (أَن جَآءَهُم).
This is a foundational particle that connects parts of a sentence. Here it introduces what surprised them - that a warner came to them. Think of it like opening quotation marks before someone speaks.
The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a hollow verb. Added fatha vowels to show past tense.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but this doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: he came to them (referring to the disbelievers mentioned later as الْكٰفِرُونَ)
steps.n: Added هُم at the end to show who received the action. This refers to the disbelievers mentioned later in the verse.

steps.m: to warn or alert about danger

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show who does the warning
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added tanween to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a' instead of 'the')
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
The م gets doubled (shadda) because it comes after a connecting word ending in ن
Added هُم at the end to show who is being referred to (them)
Added vowels to make basic past tense form
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Added و with fatha to connect this to the previous statement. This word is connected to كَذَّابٌ (liar) in this ayah.
Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he' form
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who does the action
Added ون to show it's talking about many people (plural)

steps.m: the rejecters/deniers of truth (subject of قَالَ - said)
steps.n: Added ال to specify these particular deniers, and fatha at end because it's the subject of 'said' in this ayah
Basic demonstrative pronoun form used to point to something nearby
Added alif above ه to show emphasis in pronunciation
Added ا with fatha to make it masculine singular, pointing to the word سٰحِرٌ (magician) that comes after it in the ayah
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he did magic'
Changed to describe someone who does magic. This word tells us what 'this' (هٰذَا) is.
Changed the long 'aa' to a shorter 'aa' sound and added two damma marks to show it means 'a magician' in general
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past-tense verb

steps.m: someone who often tells untrue things
steps.n: This form with shadda and alif shows someone who lies a lot
The word already has tanween - it means someone who lies a lot (like a job title)
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: did he make (the gods into one god)?
steps.n: Adding أَ at the beginning turns this into a question expressing surprise or disbelief about making many gods into one

steps.m: to worship or take as a deity

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped' by adding alif
Changed to a singular noun meaning 'a deity' by adding hamza at start
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'

steps.m: the deities (object of أَجَعَلَ - did he make)
steps.n: Changed from singular 'god' to plural 'gods' and marked as object of 'did he make'

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.n: Changed root letters into a noun pattern by adding إِ at start and ا in middle to make a word meaning 'something that is worshipped'
The alif is written as a small mark above to show the long 'aa' sound
Added ًا at the end to show this is what is being 'made/taken' in the phrase 'made the gods into a single god'
Changes from basic root to active participle form, meaning 'one who is unique/unified'
Added ًا at the end to show this word is describing إِلٰهًا (god). The وَا changed to وٰ to make it easier to say.

This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English. The doubled ن (shown by ّ) makes the emphasis even stronger. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'this is an amazing thing.'
This is the basic attention-drawing part that starts many pointing words
The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's stretched when pronounced
Adding ذَا makes it point to something specific and nearby. In this ayah, it points to what the disbelievers are saying about making many gods into one God.

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: The root letters form a noun meaning 'thing'. The middle letter ي gets a sukun (silent mark) on top.
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'indeed' or 'truly'
Added double dammah (tanween) at the end to make it indefinite
Connected the root letters with basic vowels to make a noun meaning 'amazing'
Changed vowel pattern and added ا to make the word mean 'extremely' or 'intensely' amazing
Adding fatha vowels to create basic past tense form
The ان prefix makes the verb mean 'to set out by oneself' instead of just 'to move'. It shows the person chose to start moving on their own.

steps.m: and he set out/went forth (subject of the sentence)
steps.n: The و (and) was added at the beginning to connect this action to what happened before it

steps.m: to gather, assemble (especially as a group of leaders)

steps.n: Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
Added 'the' (ال) at the start and a mark (ُ) at the end to show these chiefs/leaders are doing the action. Changed from a verb (to gather) into a noun meaning the important people themselves.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'

steps.m: from them (refers to الْمَلَأُ - the chiefs)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this is happening to 'them' (referring back to the chiefs mentioned as الْمَلَأُ earlier in the verse)
Basic particle that introduces what someone said or commanded
Added kasra at the end because it introduces a command (ٱمْشُوا۟ meaning 'walk/go'). This particle connects what the leaders said to their actual words.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى when it has a fatha.
Adding اِ at start turns it into a command to walk
Changed the ending to وا to make it a command for multiple people. Also changed the vowel before وا to damma.
Basic root meaning showing the concept of patience
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Changed to a command by adding 'i' sound at start and putting sukoon at end - tells one person to do the action
Added plural command ending to make it address multiple people
Added 'and' at beginning to connect with previous phrase
Basic form of the word showing something is on top of, upon, or about something else. In this ayah, it connects to 'your gods' (ءَالِهَتِكُمْ) to show what they should stay firm upon.
Added an alif that looks like a small line (ٰ) to show this is how the word is written in the Quran. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small vertical line (ٓ) above the word to help with proper Quranic recitation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.n: Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Adds آ at start to begin making plural form
Adds ت (ta) and kasra to show these gods belong to someone
Adds 'your (plural)' meaning by adding كُمْ at the end
Adding shadda (ّ) on the ن changes this from a conditional word to one that adds emphasis. This word emphasizes هٰذَا (this) in the ayah.
Basic pointing word that shows something is near
Added ذا to make it specifically point to a masculine thing that's near
Added alif with small alif above (ٰ) and fatha mark for proper pronunciation. This word is the subject of يُرَادُ (is intended/wanted) in this ayah.
Adding vowels to make the word pronounceable
Adds لَ to show certainty, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to passive present tense by adding يُ at start and ُ at end. This shows something is being wanted rather than someone wanting something.
This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. In this ayah, it's used with سَمِعْنَا (we heard) to say 'we have not heard'
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to hear'

steps.m: we heard (referring to what was said about هٰذَا)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. The sukun (ْ) is added before نَا to help pronunciation. This word refers to what the disbelievers heard about هٰذَا (this) in the ayah.
This combines the preposition بِ (with/by) with the demonstrative هٰذَا (this)
Basic preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it connects 'this' with 'the later religion'.
The ى at the end helps extend the sound and shows this is a complete preposition. It connects 'this' with 'the later religion' in the phrase.

steps.m: to establish or follow a path

steps.n: Changes from basic root to a noun meaning a complete system or way
Added definite article to specify this particular religion
Added kasra ending because it follows the word فِى (in) in this ayah

steps.m: to delay or come after something

steps.n: Changed the root into an active participle (doer) by adding alif after first letter, making it mean 'the one that comes after/last'
Added ة to make it feminine and turn it into a specific noun meaning 'the final life' or 'afterlife'

steps.m: the final life (afterlife) (possessed by/connected to الملة)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ِ to show it belongs to or is connected to الملة (the religion) mentioned earlier in the verse
This is a negation particle that means 'not' in this context. It's working with إِلَّا (except/only) to say 'this is nothing but an invention'
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby. The ها and ذا combine to form the word meaning 'this'.
The alif at the end is elongated (آ instead of ا) to add emphasis and make the word sound longer when read. This is common in the Quran for style and rhythm.
This is the base form of the exception particle that excludes what comes after it from what was mentioned before it

steps.m: except (excluding what comes after from وَمَا سَمِعْنَا - what we heard)
steps.n: The alif at the end makes the word connect better with what comes after it. In this ayah, it's excluding اخْتِلٰقٌ (invention/fabrication) from what they heard in the previous religion.
Basic past tense verb meaning 'he created' - added short vowels (fatha) to connect the root letters
Adding اِ and تَ changes 'create' to 'fabricate/make up falsely' - like taking the power of creation wrongly for yourself
Changes from a verb (he fabricated) to a noun meaning 'the act of fabricating'
Changed to the standard spelling with ٱ and added ٌ to show this is describing what 'this' (هٰذَآ) is
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning makes the verb causative - showing someone made something else do the action
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than something doing it

steps.m: was it sent down? (questioning the revelation of الذِّكْرُ)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question. This is asking about the revelation of الذِّكْرُ (the reminder/message) mentioned in the rest of the ayah.
The ى at the end changes to ي with sukun when adding a suffix
Added هِ at the end to show 'him'. This word connects to أُنزِلَ (was sent down) to show what was sent down upon.
Changed the action into a concrete noun - something that actually reminds or makes you remember
Adds definite article to make the noun definite
Doubles the ذ to add intensity to the meaning
Added damma to show this word is the subject of the verb أَءُنزِلَ (was it sent down?)
Basic preposition showing source or origin. In this ayah, it connects the word شَكٍّ (doubt) with ذِكْرِى (My reminder), showing they are in doubt about/from My reminder.
Added a small ن (called tanwin) at the end to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word. This doesn't change the meaning.
Forms basic noun pattern showing space or position between things
Added kasra to show this word is owned by something or follows مِن
Added نَا to show this means 'between us'

steps.m: rather/but rather (introduces a correction to أَءُنزِلَ 'was it sent down')
steps.n: This word is used to correct or contradict what came before. Here it's saying 'rather' to correct the previous question about revelation, introducing the real situation.

steps.m: they (subject of فِى شَكٍّ - are in doubt)
steps.n: Independent pronoun for 'they' (masculine plural). In this ayah, it shows who is in doubt.
Basic preposition showing location or state. In this ayah, it shows that 'they' are in a state of doubt.
Root letters ش-ك-ك combined with a shadda (doubled ك) to make the word for 'doubt'
Added tanween kasra to show this word comes after the preposition فِى (in)
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from
The shadda (ّ) on the م adds emphasis to show importance of where something comes from
Changes the verb 'to remember' into a noun meaning 'remembrance' by adding vowel marks

steps.m: but rather (introduces a statement that corrects هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ)
steps.n: This word is used to correct or contradict what was said before. In this ayah, it introduces the true state of the disbelievers - that they are in doubt - after mentioning their question about the revelation.
This is a particle word that doesn't come from a root. It's used to show something hasn't happened yet or to mean 'when' for past events.
Added the basic vowel marks and shadda to show how to pronounce the word correctly
The shadda at the beginning makes the word stronger, like saying 'definitely not yet' instead of just 'not yet'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowels

steps.m: they taste (referring to the disbelievers in شَكٍّ)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to the disbelievers mentioned earlier who are in doubt.

steps.m: punishment (what is being tasted in يَذُوقُوا)
steps.n: Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun meaning 'punishment'. This word is what is being tasted in the verb يَذُوقُوا (they taste).

steps.m: My punishment (what is being tasted in يَذُوقُوا)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this is connected to something after it (in this case, showing it's 'my punishment' based on context)
This is a connecting particle that introduces a new option or alternative in a question. In this ayah, it connects to what came before to ask 'Or do they have...?'
This is a common preposition in Arabic that comes from the idea of being present with something
Adding vowels to create the preposition form. This changes it from a concept of presence to a word showing location or possession.
Adding هُمْ shows this refers to 'them'. In this ayah, it's asking if 'they' possess the treasures (خَزَآئِنُ) mentioned after.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Changes from a verb (to store) to a noun meaning 'one who stores'
Single 'keeper' (خَازِن) changed to irregular plural 'storehouses' (خَزَآئِن) - like changing 'box' to 'boxes' but in a special Arabic way
Added ُ to show this is the subject of عِندَهُمْ (they have)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters. The fatha-sukun pattern makes it a noun.
Added ة to make it a specific instance of mercy
Added kasra to show this mercy belongs to or is connected to 'your Lord' (رَبِّكَ) that follows it in the ayah
Added fatha after first letter and doubled the middle letter with shadda to show intensity, making it mean 'lord' or 'master'
Added kasra under the last letter to show this word is owned by something else
Added كَ at the end to show this belongs to you. This word is part of the phrase 'treasures of your Lord's mercy'
Forms an intensive adjective meaning 'mighty'
Added vowels and ي to make it an intensive adjective showing strong quality

steps.m: The Mighty One (a name/attribute of Allah)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite. In this context, it's used as one of Allah's names/attributes.

steps.m: The Mighty One (attribute of Allah, connected to الْوَهَّابِ)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to الْوَهَّابِ (The Bestower) that follows it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added shadda on middle letter and alif after it to show someone who does this action intensively
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being described by another word

steps.m: The Most Giving (a name/attribute of Allah)
steps.n: Added the definite article to show this is referring to Allah's attribute of being the ultimate giver of all things
This is a question particle that connects two options in Arabic questions
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning
Added هُم to show this applies to a group of men or mixed group. This word connects to مُّلْكُ (dominion) in this ayah, showing who the dominion belongs to.
Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma (ُ) on first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter to show the result of owning/ruling
Adding shaddah (doubling mark) on م makes the meaning stronger and more intense

steps.m: ownership/dominion (subject of لَهُم - belongs to them)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of لَهُم (belongs to them) in the ayah
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the basic word for sky/heaven
Makes the word plural to mean multiple heavens
Adds ٱل to show we're talking about specific heavens
Doubles the middle letter to intensify meaning
Shows this word belongs to or is connected to مُّلْكُ (dominion/kingdom) - like saying 'dominion of the heavens'
Forms the noun meaning 'earth'
Added short 'a' and sukoon marks to make the word mean 'earth'
Added the definite article ال to specify this particular earth
Added kasra at the end to show this word follows another word in the ayah (مُّلْكُ - dominion)
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with السَّمٰوٰتِ (the heavens) mentioned before it
Combines 'and' with the question word 'what'
Added vowel marks to create the basic word meaning 'between'. The fatha and sukun pattern shows this is a word about position or location.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being used with something after it

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added هُمَا at the end to show this means 'between them both', referring to the heavens and earth mentioned earlier in the verse
Forms the basic present tense verb 'they rise'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added Form VIII pattern to show the action is done by oneself
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to plural by adding وا to show multiple people doing the action
Basic preposition showing location or containment
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to الْأَسْبٰبِ (the ways/means) to show 'in the ways/means'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic noun
Forms the broken plural pattern by adding أَ at the start and ا after the second root letter

steps.m: the causes, the reasons, the means
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific/definite
Adding kasra at the end shows this word follows فِى (in/into) in the ayah
The damma and sukun pattern makes this word mean 'a group' rather than just one soldier
Added double damma to show this is indefinite (any army). This army is described as defeated (مَهْزُومٌ) in this verse.
This is a basic word used to ask questions (like 'what?') or refer to things generally (like 'whatever')
Adding the shadda makes the word stronger, like saying 'WHAT' instead of just 'what'
This is a basic location word that points to a nearby place. It doesn't come from a root - it's a complete word by itself.
Adding لِكَ changes the meaning from 'here' to 'there' - it makes the location more distant. In this ayah, it's pointing to where the army was defeated.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters

steps.m: one who is defeated (describing جُندٌ - army)
steps.n: Changed from 'defeated' to 'defeated one' - like changing 'hit' to 'hit one'. Shows who received the action. Describes the army (جُندٌ) that was defeated.
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is any defeated one, not a specific one
This is a basic preposition that shows where something came from or what group it belongs to, like saying 'from' in English
The shadda (ّ) makes the 'm' sound stronger to emphasize the meaning
The fatha (َ) at the end connects this word to the next word الْأَحْزَابِ (the groups/confederates)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
The word for 'he gathered' changes to mean 'groups' by adding 'a' at the start and rearranging the middle letters
Adding ال makes it refer to specific groups mentioned in context
The kasra ending shows this word follows مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he strongly denied something as false (subject of قَبْلَهُمْ)
steps.n: Added shadda to make the meaning stronger. This word connects to قَبْلَهُمْ (before them) in the ayah.

steps.m: she strongly denied something as false
steps.n: Added ت with sukun at the end to show a feminine subject did the action
Added fatha and sukun to form the noun meaning 'before'. This word appears in كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ (denied before them) showing when the denial happened.

steps.m: before them (referring to the deniers mentioned in كَذَّبَتْ)
steps.n: Added pronoun هُمْ meaning 'them' to show who came before. This connects to the previous word كَذَّبَتْ (denied) to say 'denied before them'.
Added vowels to form the noun meaning 'people' - those who establish or maintain something together

steps.m: people, folk (subject of كَذَّبَتْ - they denied)
steps.n: The ُ at the end shows this word is the subject doing the denying in كَذَّبَتْ (they denied). This word is followed by نوح to show these are specifically Noah's people.
This is a proper name that doesn't come from an Arabic root. It's the name of the prophet Noah who appears in the Quran.
The tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end shows this word is being possessed by or connected to something else. Here it's connected to قَوْمُ (people) from earlier in the verse, meaning 'the people of Noah'.

steps.m: to come back or return repeatedly
and the tribe of 'Aad (subject of كَذَّبَتْ)
steps.n: Added وَ ('and') at the start to connect with the previous people mentioned. عَادٌ is the name of an ancient Arab tribe - it's not directly formed from ع-و-د.
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject (doer) in the sentence
Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this to the previous names mentioned
This is a special word that starts with the idea of pointing to something

This is incorrect. ذُو is a separate word meaning 'possessor of' and is not derived from ذ (which means 'this/that'). It's used before nouns to show ownership, like saying 'the one with' or 'owner of' something.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic noun form meaning a single stake/peg
Changed to plural by adding alif at start and alif after waw - this is a common plural pattern
Added 'the' at beginning and kasra at end to show this describes Pharaoh as 'the one of the stakes' in the ayah

steps.m: Thamud (name of an ancient Arabian tribe)
steps.n: This is a proper noun with no root - it's the name of a historical tribe mentioned in the Quran
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the previous names/tribes mentioned

steps.m: to stand firmly, establish, rise up
steps.n: Basic meaning of standing or rising
Changes verb meaning of 'standing/establishing' into noun meaning of 'established group/people'
Adds ُ to show this word is doing something in the sentence

steps.m: and the people (subject, connected to وَثَمُودُ)
steps.n: Adds وَ meaning 'and' to connect this to the previous word وَثَمُودُ (and Thamud)
This is a proper noun - the name of a prophet
The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else. Here it shows the people (قَوْمُ) belong to Lot, making the phrase 'people of Lot'.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Changes from 'he became a companion' to 'companions' by making it plural using the pattern أَفْعَال

steps.m: companions (as the doers in the sentence)
steps.n: Adds ُ to show these companions are the main people being talked about

steps.m: and companions (connected to وَثَمُودُ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ - Thamud and the people of Lot)
steps.n: Adds وَ (and) to connect this group of companions with the other groups mentioned before them
Added ة to show this is one specific thicket
Added ال to make it specific
Added kasra at the end to show this thicket belongs to or relates to something else
This is the basic form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things that are far away
The alif is elongated with a special mark (ٰ) and followed by a small alif (ٓ) for emphasis

steps.m: those (pointing to something far and addressing someone)
steps.n: Added ئِكَ at the end to show we're addressing someone about these far things
Basic meaning of gathering or grouping together
Added vowels to make the basic noun for a single group
Added hamza at start and alif between second and third letters to make plural form
Added the definite article 'the' at the start and nominative case ending
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'all' (كُلٌّ) of them rejected the messengers.
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added double damma to show this word is doing the action in the sentence
This is a basic word used to show exceptions, like saying 'but' or 'except' in English
In this ayah, إِلَّا connects 'all' (كُلٌّ) with 'denied' (كَذَّبَ) to say 'all except... denied'. It shows that everyone in a group did something, with some exceptions.
This root carries the basic meaning of denying or rejecting something as false
Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he denied'
Added shadda to intensify the meaning. In this ayah, it specifically refers to the strong rejection of Allah's messengers (الرُّسُلَ)

steps.m: to send or be sent as a messenger

steps.n: Changes from root 'send' to mean 'person who is sent' by adding vowels
Changes from رَسُول to رُسُل by dropping the long vowel و and changing the vowels to make it plural (like changing 'goose' to 'geese')
Adds definite article and doubles the ر with shadda
Adds accusative case ending to show this word is the object being denied/rejected in the phrase 'denied the messengers'
Adding vowel marks to make it a proper past tense verb

steps.m: so it became true (consequence of كَذَّبَ they denied)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happened because they denied the messengers. It connects this result to the previous action.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'punishment' (since punishment follows after wrongdoing). This word is the subject of حَقَّ (became due) in this ayah.
Adds kasra at the end to show possession or connection to something else mentioned in the ayah
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'what' when asking questions or 'not' when negating something. In this ayah, it means 'not' because it's negating what these people are waiting for.

steps.m: and not (they are waiting for يَنظُرُ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. The word negates what these people are waiting for in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. This word shows what 'these people' are doing in the ayah.
Basic attention-getting particle that starts many demonstrative pronouns. It's like saying 'look!' to get someone's attention before pointing to something.
The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's stretched out when speaking, making it more attention-grabbing
Added the plural demonstrative ending that shows we're pointing to multiple things or people that are nearby. The ءِ at the end shows it's being used with another word coming after it.

This is a basic particle used to exclude something from what was mentioned before. It connects to the previous phrase 'these people are not waiting for' to mean 'these people are not waiting for anything except...'
Adding the shadda and alif makes the exclusion stronger and complete. In this ayah, it emphasizes that they are waiting for absolutely nothing except one thing - a single blast.

steps.m: to shout or cry out loudly
steps.n: This is the basic concept of making a loud sound or cry
Changed from basic root meaning 'shout' into a single loud shout/cry by adding vowels
Added ة to show this is one specific instance of shouting
Added tanween to show this is the object of the verb يَنظُرُ (they await)
Changes from basic root to active participle form, meaning 'one who is unique/single'
Adds feminine marker to make word refer to something feminine
Changed alif to alif with overbar (وٰ) and added tanween fatha (ً) to show this describes صَيْحَةً (a cry/blast)
This is the basic form of the particle that can mean either 'what' (for questions) or 'not' (for negation)
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to show strong negation. In this ayah, it's negating the verb يَنظُرُ (they are waiting), saying 'they are not waiting'
This is a particle meaning 'to' or 'for' that doesn't come from a root
Added ها which shows this is 'for her/it'. In this ayah, it refers to the 'single blast' (صَيْحَةً) mentioned before.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects to the word فَوَاقٍ (pause/rest) to show there is no pause/rest from something.
The kasra (ِ) under the م shows this word is connecting to what comes after it
Root letters combined with vowels to make the word meaning 'above' or 'over'
Added alif to change from meaning 'above' to meaning 'a break or pause between things' (like the pause between milkings of a camel)

steps.m: any pause/delay (in the context of 'they will have no pause/delay')
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this is any general pause, used in this ayah to say there will be no pause/delay at all
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter. Added fatha marks to show it happened in the past.
Adding وا at the end changes 'he said' to 'they said', showing that a group of people are speaking
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and'

steps.m: to care for and help grow/develop
one who cares for and develops (lord)
steps.n: The doubled letter shows this is someone who completely takes care of others. The fatha shows this is a noun (person/thing) rather than an action.

steps.m: one who cares for and develops (lord)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows this word is being used in a special grammatical position

steps.m: O our lord (subject of قَالُوا/they said)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show this is our lord. This word comes after قَالُوا (they said) to show what they said.
Added basic past tense vowels
Changed to command form by adding shadda on middle letter, kasra before it, and sukun at the end
This is a combination of two parts: ل which means 'for/to' and نا which means 'us'

steps.m: for/to us (emphasizing their request to عَجِّل)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) on ل adds emphasis to show urgency in their request. This connects to the word عَجِّل (hurry) in their statement, showing they're really eager for something to happen quickly.
The root letters were combined into a noun meaning 'portion' by doubling the middle letter and adding a short 'i' sound under the first letter
Added نَا at the end to show this is our portion. This word is what is being hastened (the object) in the phrase 'hasten for us our portion'

steps.m: to be in front of something

steps.n: Changes from verb to noun meaning 'before'
Added fatha at the end because this word is possessed by يَوْمِ (day) in the phrase 'before the Day'
Connected the root letters with fatha on ya and sukun on waw to form the basic noun meaning 'day'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to الحِسَابِ (the Reckoning) that comes after it, making it mean 'day of the Reckoning'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (he counted) to a noun meaning the act of counting/calculating
Makes the noun definite to refer to a specific reckoning

steps.m: of the reckoning (possessed by يَوْمِ - day)
steps.n: Adds kasra to indicate genitive case. This word is the second term in a possession relationship (idafa) with يومِ, meaning 'day of reckoning'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to a command form by adding ٱ at start and changing vowels. This is telling one person to be patient in response to what others are saying (عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ).

steps.m: upon/on/over (connects to مَا يَقُولُونَ - what they say)
steps.n: This is a basic connecting word that shows something is on top of or above something else, or shows a relationship between things
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word - 'a' sound after ع and after ل
Added the small alif (ٰ) which shows this is the standard way to write this word in the Quran
This is the basic form of the word ما which is used to ask about or refer to things. Here it is the object of يَقُولُونَ (they say), meaning 'what they say'
Added fatha mark on م to show correct pronunciation
Added basic past tense form with fatha marks. The middle و changes to ا due to being a weak letter.
The يَ at the start shows it's happening now, not in the past
Adding ون shows multiple men are doing the action
Changes from singular (he) to plural (they) by adding ون at the end
Added sukun on first letter, damma on second letter, and sukun at end to make basic command form
Added helping alif at start to make the command easier to pronounce

steps.m: and remember! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect this command to the previous one (ٱصْبِرْ/be patient)

steps.m: to serve or be devoted to someone/something

steps.n: Root meaning 'serve' becomes 'servant' by adding marks (fatha-sukun-nothing) to the letters
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being remembered in وَٱذْكُرْ (remember)
Added نَا at the end to show this servant belongs to Us (Allah)
This is a proper name that doesn't come from a root
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the name correctly: 'Da-wu-d'
Added the small و mark (ۥ) and fatha at the end. The fatha shows this is the object of the verb 'remember' - Allah is telling us to remember David.
This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to something. It's one of the basic words in Arabic that doesn't come from a three-letter root.
This is incorrect. ذَا is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this', not 'possessor of'. The word for 'possessor of' would be ذُو. These are different words with different meanings.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'strength'
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it's connected to another word in the ayah
Added kasra under first letter and shadda on second letter to show emphasis
Added هُ (with damma) at the end to mean 'he/him/it'
Added pronunciation helpers: ۥ to show the 'hu' sound is rounded and ٓ (alif khanjariya) to show slight elongation of the final vowel
Added shadda on و and alif after it to show someone who does this action a lot
Added tanwin dammah to show this is describing Dawud (David) in a general way
This is a word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: indeed we (subject of سَخَّرْنَا - we subjected)
steps.n: Added ا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This connects to the next word سَخَّرْنَا (we subjected/made subservient)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger - showing complete control
Changed final vowel to sukun to prepare for suffix

steps.m: We completely controlled (the mountains mentioned in ٱلْجِبَالَ)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show 'we' did this action. This word connects to 'the mountains' (ٱلْجِبَالَ) which comes after it in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic singular noun
Changed vowel marks and added alif to show many mountains
Added ال to make it definite and َ at the end to show it's the object of the verb سَخَّرْنَا (We subjected/made subservient)
This is a preposition showing accompaniment

steps.m: with him (connected to يُسَبِّحْنَ - they glorify)
steps.n: Added هُ to show it means 'him'. The ۥ is just a pronunciation mark. This word connects to يُسَبِّحْنَ (they glorify) to tell us who is being glorified with.
Doubling the middle letter shows doing something with extra effort or repeatedly
Adding يُ at the beginning shows the action is happening now or regularly

steps.m: they (feminine) glorify intensely (subject of the mountains in إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا ٱلْجِبَالَ)
steps.n: Changed to feminine plural to match 'the mountains' which are treated as feminine plural in Arabic. This word describes what the mountains do in praise of Allah.
Adding shadda at end makes it mean 'time of evening'
Added ال to make it specific - 'the evening' instead of just any evening

steps.m: in the evening (time when mountains praise with him)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this happens during evening time. The alif changes to hamzat wasl (ٱ) after بِ. This word connects to the previous part where the mountains praise with him.
Changed the root into a noun that describes the action or result of shining
Makes the noun definite
Added و (and) to connect this with the previous word بِٱلْعَشِىِّ (in the evening)

steps.m: and the shining (of sunrise) (connected to بِٱلْعَشِىِّ)
steps.n: The ِ shows this word is connected to the earlier phrase بِٱلْعَشِىِّ (in the evening), making the complete phrase mean 'in the evening and at sunrise'
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to form the basic noun
Adding definite article 'al' and shadda on first letter due to sun letter rule
Adding و at beginning to connect with previous phrase. This bird is being gathered (محشورة) along with other things under David's control.
Added fatha to show this is the object being gathered (محشورة). The birds are being described as gathered together in the next word.
Forms passive participle indicating the birds that have been gathered together

steps.m: gathered (feminine, describing الطير which is feminine)
steps.n: Adds feminine marker because الطير (the birds) is treated as feminine in this context

steps.m: gathered (feminine, as a state/description of الطير)
steps.n: Adds tanween (double fatha) to show this is describing the state or condition of the birds
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added double damma (ٌ) at the end to show this word is the subject doing the action in the sentence
This is the basic preposition showing direction or purpose
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning
Added the pronoun 'him' to show who it's for
Added pronunciation marks ۥٓ to show how to elongate the sound
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make the past tense verb 'he returned'
Added shadda and alif to show this describes someone who does this action very frequently. This word describes كُلٌّ (each) in the ayah.

steps.m: one who constantly returns (to Allah) (describing كُلٌّ)
steps.n: Adding tanwin dammah shows this is describing كُلٌّ (each) in the ayah
Added fathas to connect the root letters into a past tense verb

steps.m: we strengthened (acted on مُلْكَهُۥ - his kingdom)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word is connected to مُلْكَهُۥ (his kingdom) which comes right after it in the ayah - that's what was strengthened.

steps.m: and we strengthened (acted on مُلْكَهُۥ - his kingdom)
steps.n: Added وَ prefix meaning 'and'
Changed the word from an action (controlling) into a thing (kingdom) by adding special vowel marks
Added fatha to show this word is receiving the action of شَدَدْنَا (We strengthened)
Added هُ at the end to show the kingdom belongs to him
Connected the root letters with fatha on middle letter

steps.m: to give, cause to come (acted on)
steps.n: Changed to Form IV pattern to show causative meaning (making someone receive something)
Added نَا to show 'we' as the subject, with sukun on ي to connect the sounds
Changed نَا to نٰ and added هُ (him) to show who received the action
Added وَ (and) to connect this action with the previous statement (وَشَدَدْنَا مُلْكَهُۥ - and We strengthened his kingdom)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed the vowels and added ة to make it mean the quality or result of judging wisely
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and َ to show it's the object being given in the phrase 'We gave him the wisdom'

steps.m: to separate or decide between things clearly

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun that means the act or result of separating/deciding
Added 'and' at the beginning and made it an object of the verb 'We gave him' from earlier in the verse
Added ا after second root letter and vowel marks to form a noun meaning the result of the action
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it follows فَصْلَ (decisive judgment of). Together they mean 'decisive judgment of formal speech/discourse'
This is a question word that doesn't come from a root

steps.m: and has/did (asking about أَتَىٰكَ - came to you)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this question with what came before. This word is asking about the verb أَتَىٰكَ (came to you) in this ayah.
Added fatha marks to show past tense and connected the letters
Added ك with fatha at the end to show the action was directed to 'you'
This is the basic noun form meaning 'news'
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make the basic noun form
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence. The hamza (ء) changes to (ؤ) after damma.
Added alif at end to show multiple people did the action

steps.m: to oppose or dispute with someone
steps.n: This forms a noun meaning 'disputant' or 'opponent'
Root letters got vowel marks to make a noun meaning 'someone who argues/disputes'

steps.m: the opponent (part of the story/news mentioned in نَبَؤُا۟)
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning to make it specific (like 'the' in English) and ِ at the end to show it's connected to the word نَبَؤُا۟ (news/story) - like saying 'story of the opponent'
This is a time word that doesn't come from a root. It connects the story about to be told (when they climbed) to the previous question (has the story reached you?)
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda on middle letter shows doing the action with intensity
Adding تَ at the beginning shows doing the action to oneself
Adding وا at the end shows a group did the action
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Added مِ at start and ا in middle to create a noun for a place. Even though the root means 'fight', this pattern creates a special meaning for a sacred place or private chamber.
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the place of prayer/worship (object of تَسَوَّرُوا)
steps.n: Added fatḥa to show this is what was climbed over (object of 'they climbed over' تَسَوَّرُوا in the ayah)
This is a time word that tells us when something happened in the past. Here it introduces the story of when some people entered upon Dawud.
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to show this happened in the past

steps.m: they entered (subject of فَفَزِعَ - so he became afraid)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show a group of people did the action. This group entering made David (داود) become afraid in the next part of the verse.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something is physically above something else
The alif at the end is written with a special mark (ىٰ) to show it's being used in an abstract way. Here it shows opposition or aggression against David, not physical location above him.
This is the basic form of the proper name David, borrowed from Hebrew. In Arabic, it's treated as a non-Arabic proper noun that doesn't follow normal Arabic word patterns.

steps.m: David (as the object being entered upon in this ayah)
steps.n: Added a fatha at the end to show that David is the object being entered upon (they entered upon David). The small و (waw) mark is added above the و to show proper pronunciation.

steps.m: to become suddenly afraid or startled
he became suddenly afraid (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to show this happened in the past. This word describes his reaction right before they spoke (قَالُوا).

steps.m: and so he became suddenly afraid (of them)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened as a result of them entering. The fear was specifically of them (مِنْهُمْ comes right after in the ayah).
Basic preposition showing something comes from or originates from somewhere

steps.m: from them (referring to those who entered)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this refers to 'them' - the people who entered mentioned earlier in the ayah (إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟)
Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. The middle letter و changes to ا which is common in Arabic words

steps.m: they said (referring to the group who entered upon داود)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show a group of people (they) did the action. This connects to the previous word دَخَلُوا (they entered) showing it's the same group doing both actions.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb تَخَفْ (fear) into 'do not fear'. It's commonly used before verbs to tell someone not to do something.
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation
Added fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at the beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Removed the ا and added sukun to make it a command. This is a common pattern with hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it into a noun for someone who argues or disputes
Added انِ to show there are exactly two people disputing. This refers to the two people who came to Prophet David (داود) to resolve their argument.
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The final ya changes to alif maqsurah because this is a weak verb
Basic noun form showing part of something
The word 'some' (بَعْض) got 'our/us' (نَا) attached to it, making it mean 'some of us'. The ـُ (damma) at the end shows it's doing the action.
Basic preposition showing that someone entered upon/came to David. In Arabic, we use عَلَى (upon) where English would use 'to' for visiting someone.
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show the proper pronunciation of the long vowel sound. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to separate or divide something into parts

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'a part' instead of the action of dividing
Added tanween kasra at the end because it comes after عَلَىٰ (upon/against) in the phrase عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ (against a part)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he judged' to 'judge!' by adding alif at start and changing vowels to make a command
Added فَ to show this command comes as a result of what was said before (when they asked David to judge between them)

steps.m: to separate or be distinct from

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to separate' into noun meaning 'between'
Added نَا to show this is happening between 'us' (the two disputing parties speaking to David). This whole phrase is what David is being asked to judge.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
The doubled letter with shadda makes it into a noun meaning the concept itself
Adding ال makes it specific - this particular truth/right

steps.m: with the truth (used with فَٱحْكُم - so judge)
steps.n: Adding بِ shows how the judging should be done - with/by the truth. This connects to the command فَٱحْكُم (so judge) in the ayah.
Basic negative particle that negates the verb that follows it
Added 'and' at the beginning to connect this prohibition with the previous command فَٱحْكُم (judge/rule). The full meaning is 'so judge between us with truth and do not exceed bounds'
This root carries the basic meaning of going beyond what is right or fair
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he did' to 'you do' by removing first vowel and changing middle vowel, with silent ending to make it a command
Adding تُ changes it from command to 'you' form

steps.m: to guide or direct to the right path

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb. The final ي becomes ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Changed to command form by adding اِ at start and changing vowels. The final weak letter is dropped in command form.
Added نَا to show who is being guided (us)
Added وَ (and) at start and changed اِ to ٱ because it comes after و
Changed final alif to long alif (آ) following Quranic writing style
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it's used with وَٱهْدِنَآ (guide us) to show where the guidance should lead.
The alif at the end is written with a small mark above it (called an alif maqsurah). This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to be level, straight, or equal

steps.n: Changes from verb to noun meaning 'equality/sameness'
Added kasra to show this describes الصِّرٰطِ (the path) that comes after it in the ayah

steps.m: to make a path or way

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun by adding vowels and alif. This makes it mean the actual path rather than the action of making a path.
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning to make it specific. The ص gets a shadda because it's a sun letter.
Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by سَوَآءِ (middle/even), forming the phrase 'middle of the path'. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this style.

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes هٰذَآ أَخِى - 'this is my brother')
steps.n: This is a special word that makes what comes after it more certain and emphasized. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'this is my brother' is definitely true.
This is the basic pointing word used to indicate something nearby
Added هٰ at the beginning to make the pointing stronger and clearer

steps.m: this (pointing to something near) (brother of أَخِى)
steps.n: The alif is lengthened to آ to help with pronunciation. This word is pointing to the brother (أَخِى) mentioned right after it in the ayah.
The root letters أ-خ-و combine together to make the simple word 'brother'. The و at the end gets dropped - this happens a lot in Arabic with و at the end of words.
Adding the kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word is being used as an object of a preposition or possession
Adding ى at the end shows this is 'my' brother. This word is the subject of لَهُۥ (he has) in this ayah.
Added fatha to show the basic meaning of direction or possession
Added هُ to show who the action is for (in this case, referring to the brother mentioned as أَخِى in the ayah)

steps.m: he has (referring to the brother who has 99 ewes)
steps.n: Added the small و mark (ۥ) to help with pronunciation. In this context, لَهُۥ means 'he has' when followed by the number of ewes (تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً)
Basic root letters got vowel marks (ـِ and ـْ) to make the number nine
Added two dots (tanween) above to show this is the first part of the number 'ninety-nine'
Added vowel marks to make the basic number 'nine'
Added ون to make it plural, meaning 'ninety'
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make a basic noun
Added ة to specify this is a female sheep

steps.m: a female sheep/ewe (as the object being discussed)
steps.n: Added ً (tanween) to show this female sheep is the direct object - the thing being acted upon
Added kasra to show this is a possession particle
Added ى with fatha to show this means 'I' as the subject
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. This word connects to نَعْجَةٌ (sheep) which comes after it in the ayah.
Added short vowels (fat-ha and sukoon) to make the basic word for 'sheep'
Added ة to make it specifically feminine and singular
Added tanween dammah to make it indefinite (a sheep, not the sheep)
Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb
Changed the middle vowel and added alif to make it mean 'something that is one/alone' instead of 'was alone'
Added ة to make it feminine since it's describing نَعْجَة (ewe/female sheep) in the ayah
Added tanween dammah to make it indefinite - 'a' single thing rather than 'the' single thing
Added vowels to make it past tense. This shows the action happened in the past.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter. This is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle.

steps.m: so he said (connected to previous statement)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this statement follows from what came before. In this ayah, it connects to the previous statement about the number of sheep.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from past tense 'he took care of' to a command 'give me care of!'
Added نِي to show who the command affects (me)

steps.m: put her in my care (referring to the one sheep mentioned)
steps.n: Added هَا meaning 'her/it' to refer to the نَعْجَة (sheep) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: he overpowered (subject of فَقَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added the shadda (ّ) to show the doubled middle letter. This word is part of what was said in فَقَالَ (he said).
The ن is added before ى as a connecting consonant to prevent two vowels from coming together. The ى pronoun suffix indicates the direct object 'me'
وَ is the conjunction meaning 'and'. The context shows this overpowering happened in an argument or debate.
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects to the word الْخِطَابِ (speech/conversation) to show 'in speech'

steps.m: to speak to someone/address someone formally

steps.n: Added ا after second root letter and vowels to form a noun meaning the act or result of speaking formally
Added ٱلْ at beginning to make it specific and ِ at end because it follows فِى (in)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is common for hollow verbs
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation of this emphasis word. قَدْ is used to show certainty about something that happened.

steps.m: certainly indeed (emphasizing what ظَلَمَكَ says after)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis. This combination is very common in the Quran when strongly confirming something. Here it emphasizes the statement 'he has wronged you' (ظَلَمَكَ) that follows.
Added fathas (َ) to connect the letters and show this is something that happened in the past

steps.m: he wronged you (referring to one of two disputants speaking to داود)
steps.n: Added ك at the end to show who was wronged (you). This comes from one person complaining to Prophet David about being wronged by his brother.
Changed the root into a noun that means the act of asking
Added بِ at the beginning to show how something was done, and added ِ at the end to show connection to what follows (your ewe)
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters
Added ة to show this is one specific female sheep
Added -ti at the end to show 'of' (possession)
Added ك to show this belongs to the person being addressed
This is a preposition with no root. It shows direction or destination.

Added an alif that looks like a small line (ىٰ) at the end. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning. In this ayah, it connects to the word نِعَاجِهِ (his ewes) to show 'to his ewes'.
Connected the root letters together to form the basic word for a female sheep
Changed the word to show multiple female sheep

steps.m: his female sheep (owned by the one with your single ewe)
steps.n: Added 'hi' (meaning 'his') at the end to show who owns the sheep
This is an emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows. The shadda (double letter mark) makes it stronger.
The و connects this statement to what came before it, adding the meaning of 'and'
Added vowels and ي to make a word meaning 'many' or 'much'
Added ًا to show how the transgression happens - it happens 'very much'. This describes the verb يَبْغِى (transgress) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it belongs to. Here it connects to الْخُلَطَآءِ (the partners/associates)
The shadda (ّ) makes the م sound stronger, which happens when certain letters come before مِن
The fatha (َ) at the end helps connect this word smoothly to الْخُلَطَآءِ (the partners/associates) that comes after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed vowels and added اء to create plural noun for people who share or mix together
Added the definite article to specify these particular partners
Added kasra and lengthened alif to show this word follows مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah

steps.m: to wrongfully seek more than one's right

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fathas) to make it 'he transgressed' in the past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added لَ at beginning to show emphasis/certainty
The root letters were given vowel marks to make the word mean 'some/part' and show it's doing the action

steps.m: some of them (as subject of يَبْغِى - transgresses)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who the 'some' refers to. This word is the subject of يَبْغِى (transgresses) in this ayah.
Basic preposition showing one thing being above or against another. Here it connects 'some of them' to 'others'.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: 'a' sound (َ) after ع and 'a' sound (َ) after ل
Added small alif (ٰ) after ى to show it's pronounced with a long 'aa' sound at the end
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the basic noun form
Added tanwin kasra to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence (after عَلَىٰ meaning 'against')
This is a basic exception particle in Arabic that isn't built from a root. It's like saying 'but' or 'except' in English.
Adding shadda and alif makes the word stronger and clearer. In this ayah, it introduces an exception to the previous statement about 'many' (كَثِيرًا) partners being unjust.
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people or things
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (believed) that comes right after it, describing who did the believing.
Added the proper pronunciation marks: the hamza at the start becomes a wasl hamza (ٱ), a shadda on the ل, kasra under ذ, and fatha on the ن
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added a long alif after the hamza to mean 'to believe/have faith' instead of just 'trust'
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word describes 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) from the previous part of the verse.

steps.m: to work, to do good deeds

steps.n: Changed from basic root to past tense verb by adding the vowels 'a' after each letter
The suffix وا indicates masculine plural (they) in past tense
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters

Adding alif after first root letter creates active participle pattern فاعل indicating 'one who does the action'. This word is the object (in accusative case) of the verb عَمِلُوا meaning 'they did/performed'. So together it means 'they performed righteous deeds'.
Adding ات at the end changes it from 'one righteous person' to 'many righteous things/deeds'
Adding ال makes it definite
Changed alif to waṣla (ٱ), added kasra ending to show it's the object, and elongated alif to ٰ

steps.m: little, few (describing the believers mentioned before)
steps.n: Forms an adjective describing how many of the believers there are

steps.m: few/little ones (referring to the believers who don't wrong others)
steps.n: The tanween (ٌ) shows this is describing an undefined group

steps.m: and few/little ones (connecting to 'those who believe' إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟)
steps.n: The و connects this description to the previous phrase about believers who do good deeds
This is the basic form of the word ما which is used to mean 'that which' or sometimes asks 'what?' in questions
The shadda (ّ) is added to emphasize the connection between this word and قَلِيلٌ (few). Together they mean 'how few they are'

steps.m: they/them (referring to the business partners/خُلَطَآء mentioned earlier)
steps.n: This is a pronoun that refers to a group of males or mixed group. In this ayah, it specifically refers back to the business partners (خُلَطَآء) mentioned earlier who wrong each other.
Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
Added و at beginning to connect this action to what came before. This word is connected to what Dawud did next - he sought forgiveness (فَٱسْتَغْفَرَ).
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew (דָּוִד/David). It doesn't follow regular Arabic root patterns.
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence - David is the one who thought.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger, like saying 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Adding مَا makes the meaning stronger and more specific. In this ayah, it connects to the word ظَنَّ (thought) to show what Dawud thought.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Adding shadda doubles the middle letter to show the action was done strongly or repeatedly
Adding ا at the end changes it from 'he' to 'we'
Adding هُ shows who received the action (him)
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
Added است at the beginning to show seeking or requesting forgiveness

steps.m: and then he sought forgiveness (from رَبَّهُ - his Lord)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this action followed what came before. This word connects to رَبَّهُ (his Lord) which comes right after it in the ayah.

steps.m: master who cares for others (being acted upon)
steps.n: Root ر-ب-ب became رَبَّ (master/lord) with a fatha at the end because it's the object - someone is asking forgiveness from their Lord

steps.m: his master who cares for him (being asked forgiveness from)
steps.n: Added هُ to show this is David's Lord that he's asking forgiveness from
Added fathas to connect the root letters and make it past tense
The shadda doubles the ر to intensify the meaning of falling

steps.m: and he fell down heavily (followed by رَاكِعًا - in prostration)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect to previous action. This word is followed by رَاكِعًا which explains how he fell down - in prostration.

steps.m: to bow down in prayer or submission
one who is bowing down (in prayer)
steps.n: Added ا after ر and kasra under ك to show this describes someone doing the action
Added ًا at the end to show this describes the state/way in which David fell (خَرَّ)

steps.m: and he turned back in repentance (after فَٱسْتَغْفَرَ)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to show this happened after seeking forgiveness. This completes the sequence: he sought forgiveness AND turned back to Allah.

steps.m: to forgive, to cover over faults

steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the forgiving. The ْ (sukun) is added before نا to help pronunciation

steps.m: so/then we forgave (connected to previous events)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this forgiveness happened as a result of what came before
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter lam

steps.m: for him/to him (connected to فَغَفَرْنَا - so we forgave)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُ (him) with damma to show it's connected to the word before it. This shows who is being forgiven in the phrase 'so we forgave him'
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper showing that the هُ should be pronounced with a long 'u' sound
Basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for something close by
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called an overbar
Added ل with kasra (ِ) to show distance - changes 'this' to 'that'
Added ك with fatha (َ) to show you're talking to someone
Basic emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English. The shadda (ّ) makes it stronger.

steps.m: and indeed (connects to previous statement about forgiveness)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this statement to the previous one about Allah forgiving David. The و here shows this is an additional blessing being mentioned.
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced

steps.m: for him (connected to فَغَفَرْنَا - so we forgave)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُ meaning 'him' to show who the action is for. This connects to the verb فَغَفَرْنَا (so we forgave) to show who was forgiven.
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
This is a common preposition in Arabic that comes from the idea of being present with something
Adding kasra makes this into a preposition showing location or possession
Adding fatha helps connect to following words
Adding نَا shows this is 'with us'. This word connects to لَزُلْفَىٰ (nearness) in the ayah, showing he has nearness with us.

steps.m: to come close or draw near

steps.n: Added damma and sukun to make it into a noun meaning the state of being close
Added ى at the end to make it feminine and emphasize its special nature
Added dagger alif above the alif maksura to show proper pronunciation
The لَ prefix adds emphasis, like saying 'surely' or 'certainly'
Changed to a noun showing the quality of excellence. This word connects to مَـَٔابٍ (place of return) to form the phrase 'excellence of return'
Added و to connect this phrase to what came before, showing another blessing given (after لَزُلْفَىٰ)

steps.m: to return back to where you started

steps.n: Adding مَ at the beginning turns this into a word for the place where the action happens, like how 'sleep' becomes 'bedroom' (a place for sleeping) in English

steps.m: a place of return (connected to حُسْنَ - excellence)
steps.n: Two changes happen here: 1) The و changes to ا because it's a weak letter, and 2) Adding ٍ at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to another word (حُسْنَ meaning 'excellence')
This is the proper name David (داود). The و has a small و (ۥ) above it to show it's pronounced clearly.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the main subject being addressed
Added يٰ (yaa) at the beginning to call out to David directly. This is like saying 'Hey David!' or 'O David!' in English.
This is a basic particle that adds emphasis to a statement, like adding 'indeed' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence in English
Added نا at the end as 'we', which shows this is about multiple speakers. This word connects to جَعَلْنٰكَ (made you) in the ayah, where 'we' is doing the action of making.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Changed final vowel to sukun to prepare for attachments
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action
Added كَ at the end to show who was made (you). The alif in نا is written as ٰ when كَ is added.

steps.m: to come after, take someone's place, or succeed
steps.n: This is the basic meaning showing the idea of following or replacing someone
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters and ة at the end to make it mean 'one who succeeds/comes after'

steps.m: a successor (object of جَعَلْنٰكَ - we made you)
steps.n: Added tanween (ً) to show this is the object of 'we made you' (جَعَلْنٰكَ) in the ayah
This is a preposition showing location or containment. Here it shows where the successor/authority (خَلِيفَةً) is placed - in the earth.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun
Makes the noun definite by adding the definite article
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after the word فِى (in) in the ayah

steps.m: to judge or make wise decisions

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command form telling one person to do something
Added helper alif at start to make it easier to pronounce
Added فَ at start to connect it with the previous statement, showing this command is a result of what came before
Changes from verb 'to be between' into the noun 'between'
Adds the final vowel mark to show this word's role in connecting parts of the sentence

steps.m: people (those who are familiar with each other)
steps.n: Changes from verb 'to be familiar' to noun meaning 'people'
Added 'the' at the beginning. The ن gets a shadda (double mark) because it combines with the ل from ال
Added ِ at the end because this word comes after بَيْنَ (between) in the ayah
The root letters combine with a shadda (double mark) on the middle letter to form a noun meaning 'truth'

steps.m: with the truth (describing how to judge فَٱحْكُم)
steps.n: Added بِ (with/by) at start and ال (the) before the word, plus kasra at end because it follows بِ. This word explains how the judging (فَٱحْكُم) should be done - with truth and justice.
Adding بِ shows this is describing how something is done - with/by means of truth
This is a negative particle that negates the verb that follows it, making it mean 'do not'
Adding وَ connects this command to the previous one (فَٱحْكُم - so judge), making it part of a series of instructions
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added تَ at start to show 'you/she' is doing the action now
Added shadda to show stronger following
The kasra at the end shows this is a command or request
Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb

steps.m: the desire/whim (that leads away from سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific. In this ayah, it refers to the desires that lead people away from Allah's path (سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ).

steps.m: the desire/whim (that leads away from سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added alif maksura (ىٰ) to show this is the standard written form. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to stray from the right path

steps.n: Changed from basic root to past tense verb by adding vowels and doubling the last letter
Changed to present tense causative form - showing someone causing another to stray
Added فَ to show this is a result of what came before

steps.m: so he leads you astray (from سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - the path of Allah)
steps.n: Added كَ (meaning 'you') to show who is being led astray, and changed the ending to match the grammar after فَ (so/then)
This is a preposition that shows something moving away from or leaving something else. In this ayah, it shows people straying away from Allah's path (سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ).

steps.m: to walk, travel, or make a path

steps.n: Changes from basic root to noun meaning a physical or spiritual path
The kasra at the end shows this word belongs to Allah (اللَّهِ) in this ayah
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to the One True God.

steps.m: of Allah (possessed by/connected to سَبِيلِ - path)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this is possessed by or connected to something else (the path/way). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
Adding shadda (ّ) changes this from a conditional 'if' to an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. Here it emphasizes the people (ٱلَّذِينَ) who are being described.
This is a relative pronoun used to describe a group of people - like saying 'the ones who' or 'those who'

steps.m: he wandered off the right path
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added fatha marks. The doubled ل is shown with a shadda because the last two root letters are the same.

steps.m: he wanders off the right path
steps.n: Changes from past tense (he went astray) to present tense (he goes astray) by adding يَ at the start. The doubled ل stays in both forms.

steps.m: they wander off the right path
steps.n: Adds masculine plural suffix to indicate multiple male subjects
This is a basic preposition showing someone/something being led away from the right path. In this ayah, it connects 'lead you astray' (يُضِلَّكَ) with 'path' (سَبِيلِ).

steps.m: to travel or move along a path

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels changes it from a verb into a noun meaning 'path'
Added kasra at the end because it's connected to the next word (اللَّهِ), showing it belongs to or is of Allah
