Added shadda to emphasize the L sound, showing this is a special name

steps.m: of Allah (possessed by/connected to سَبِيلِ - path)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this belongs to or is connected to something (in this case, the word سَبِيلِ meaning 'path')
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: for them (who stray from سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show this applies to 'them' (referring to those who stray from Allah's path mentioned in the previous part of the verse)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun that describes the action. This word is the subject in the sentence 'they have a severe punishment', where لَهُمْ means 'they have' or 'for them'.
Adding tanween dammah (ٌ) makes this an indefinite noun. This word is the subject in the phrase لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ (they have a punishment)
Forms an adjective describing the punishment mentioned in the ayah (عَذَابٌ)
Adding double dammah makes the word indefinite - meaning 'a severe punishment' rather than 'the severe punishment'
Combines the preposition ب (with/by) with ما (what)
Basic past tense verb meaning 'forgot'
Changed the vowels and ending to show multiple people did the action. The weak letter ي changes to و before the plural ending.
This word is part of the phrase 'Day of Reckoning', where يَوْمَ (day) is connected to الحِسَابِ (the Reckoning)
Changes from a verb (to calculate) into a noun (calculation)
Added ال to make it specific and ِ to show it belongs to يَوْمَ (the Day), forming 'Day of Reckoning'
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'what' or 'not'. In this ayah, it means 'what' because it's followed by خَلَقْنَا (we created), asking about what was created.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English.

steps.m: to create, form, or shape something
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of creating or bringing something into existence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is something that happened in the past. This word follows وَمَا (and not) in this ayah.
Added نَا at the end to show that 'We' (Allah) did the action. The ْ (sukun) is added before نَا to help pronunciation. This word follows وَمَا (and not) in this ayah.
This root relates to height and elevation
Root letters connected with vowels to make past tense 'he/it rose up'
Changed from verb 'to rise' into noun 'sky' by adding ء (hamza) at the end
Added ٱل to make it definite and doubled the س with shadda
Added fatha at end to show this is the object being created in the phrase 'We created the sky'
This root relates to earth and ground
The root letters get vowels to make it a regular noun meaning 'earth'. This is what got created in the sentence.
Adding a sukun (ْ) on the middle letter makes it a basic noun pattern that's easy to pronounce
Added 'the' (ال) to make it specific, with a small mark (sukun) on ل
Adding conjunction و with fatha to connect to next word
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'what' (for questions) or 'not' (for negation). In this ayah, it means 'what' since it's asking about what is between the heaven and earth.

steps.m: and what (connects to خَلَقْنَا - we created)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. This connects to the verb خَلَقْنَا (we created) to ask 'and what we created'.
This root carries the basic idea of separation or distinction between things
Adding vowels changes this from a root into a common preposition meaning 'between'
Added هُمَا at the end to show this means 'between them both', referring to the sky and earth mentioned before this word
Basic noun form
Forms active participle pattern فَاعِل showing something characterized by being false or useless
Adding double dammah makes it indefinite - meaning 'a false thing' rather than 'the false thing'
Adding ًا at the end changes this into an adverb showing how something is done
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
Added ل with kasra to make it more formal, like saying 'that one' instead of just 'that'
Added كَ at the end to show we're talking about something masculine and singular
Basic verb form
Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it 'he thought' in the past tense
Combined the doubled letter with shadda and removed final vowel to make it a noun
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of ذٰلِكَ (that is)
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people or things

steps.m: I (in the context of 'I am only a warner')

steps.m: to warn or give notice of danger
one who warns (subject of أَنَا۠/I am)
steps.n: Adding مُ at the start and ِ in the middle turns this into a noun meaning 'someone who warns others'. This word describes what the speaker (I) is in this ayah.

This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous word مُنذِرٌ (warner)

steps.m: from/of (connecting إِلٰهٍ to what comes before)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing source or origin. In this ayah, it connects to the word إِلٰهٍ (god/deity) to show there is no source/origin of deity except Allah.

Added a small alif (ٰ) after the second letter to elongate the vowel sound
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite and follows the word مِنْ (from/of) in the ayah
This is a basic exception word that shows something is left out from what was mentioned before. It's like saying 'but not' or 'other than' in English.

Adding the shadda (ّ) and alif (ا) makes the exception stronger, showing that ONLY this thing is different from everything else mentioned. In this ayah, it's emphasizing that there is absolutely no god EXCEPT Allah.
Changed the concept of worship into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added the nominative case ending to show this is the subject
Changes from basic root to active participle form, meaning 'one who is unique/unified'
Adding ال makes it specific and the ُ shows this is one of Allah's names being used as a subject. This appears with القَهَّارُ (The Overpowering) to emphasize Allah's absolute uniqueness and power.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter and alif show this is someone who overpowers to the highest degree
Adding ال makes this refer to Allah specifically as The Supreme Overpowerer

steps.m: The Supreme Overpowerer (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: The ُ shows this is describing Allah directly in this ayah

steps.m: to care for, nurture, raise up
Lord (the one who nurtures and cares for all)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) shows this is someone who does this action perfectly and completely

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated

steps.n: Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high up
This is how we make it plural when talking about more than one sky/heaven
Adding ٱل makes it specific, like saying 'the heavens' instead of just any heavens
Added ِ at the end to show this belongs to something (رَبُّ - Lord of)
Added short vowels (fat-ha and sukoon) to make it mean 'the earth' as a specific thing rather than just the general concept

steps.m: the earth (connected to رَبُّ - Lord of)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ِ to show it belongs to رَبُّ (Lord)

steps.m: and the earth (connected to ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ - the heavens)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect it with ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ (the heavens) mentioned before it
This is a relative pronoun that refers to things rather than people

steps.m: and what (connects رَبُّ ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ with what's between them)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with the previous one about 'Lord of the heavens'. The word 'what' here refers to everything that exists between the heavens and earth.

steps.m: to be separate or distinct from something

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to separate' into noun meaning 'between'

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past-tense verb
This form shows someone who has this quality in an intense way

steps.m: The Mighty One (a name of Allah)

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The shadda (ّ) on the middle letter and alif after it show this is someone who forgives again and again

steps.m: The Oft-Forgiving One (attribute of اللَّهِ, subject of this phrase)
steps.n: The ُ ending shows this is one of Allah's names being used as a subject in this phrase
Added damma (ُ) on first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter to make it a command

steps.n: This is a standalone pronoun that refers to what is being commanded to be told. In this ayah, it connects what is being told (قُلْ - say!) to what follows (نَبَؤٌا۟ عَظِيمٌ - a great news).
Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
The hamza (ء) changes to (ؤ) because it comes after a vowel and before damma

Added vowels and ي to create an adjective showing a strong, inherent quality
Adds tanween dammah (ٌ) to show this is describing the 'news' (نَبَؤٌا۟) in a general way
This is the basic form of the pronoun used to address someone directly. It doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: you all (who are turning away from عَنْهُ)
steps.n: Adding م at the end makes this address multiple people instead of just one. In this ayah, it refers to the people who are turning away (مُعْرِضُونَ) from something (عَنْهُ).

This is a preposition that shows either physical distance ('away from') or what topic is being discussed ('about'). In this ayah, it's used with مُعْرِضُونَ (turning away) to show what they are turning away from.

steps.m: away from him/about him (object of مُعْرِضُونَ - turning away)
steps.n: Added هُ ('him') at the end to show who the preposition refers to. This connects to مُعْرِضُونَ (turning away) to show what they are turning away from.
Adding مُ at the start and ِ under the ر shows this describes someone who does the action of turning away

steps.m: those who turn away (subject of أَنتُمْ)
steps.n: The ون ending makes it plural masculine (more than two males) and shows it describes the pronoun أَنتُمْ (you all)

steps.m: what (asking about something in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a question word asking about something unknown. In this ayah, it's asking 'what knowledge?' about the highest assembly.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows the basic form before the weak letter changes.

steps.m: he was (negated by مَا before it)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels. This is a common change in Arabic that makes the word easier to say. The word is negated by مَا before it, making it mean 'was not'.

steps.n: Added tanwin kasratayn (ٍ) at the end to show this is 'any knowledge' rather than specific knowledge. This word connects to what comes after it (بِٱلْمَلَإِ) to show what the knowledge is about.

steps.m: you were (addressing Iblis in response to his refusal to prostrate)
steps.n: Added تَ to show this is talking about 'you' (masculine singular) in the past. This is addressing Iblis in Allah's question about why he refused to prostrate.
This is the basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else or is part of something
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word الْعَالِينَ (the arrogant ones). This is like adding a connecting sound to make the words flow better when speaking.
Added ا after first root letter and ِ after second root letter to create active participle form showing someone/something that has this quality
Adds ين at the end to make it plural (more than one)
Added ال at beginning to make it specific. This word comes after مِنَ (from) in the phrase مِنَ ٱلْعَالِينَ (from among the arrogant/lofty ones).
Added vowel marks to make the basic past tense form. This shows the action happened in the past and was done by 'he'.

steps.m: he said (introducing what Iblis/Satan says next)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because قول is a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning. This word introduces the direct speech that follows.
This is the basic form of the personal pronoun meaning 'I'. It doesn't come from a root since it's one of the fundamental pronouns in Arabic.
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word clearly - أَ (a) and نَ (na).
Added a special mark (۠) that shows this is a complete word. In this ayah, this pronoun is used by Iblis to refer to himself when speaking to Allah, saying 'I am better than him.'

steps.m: to create or form something from nothing

steps.m: from (showing what نَّارٍ/fire is made of)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows the source or origin of something. In this ayah, it's used twice - first to show Iblis was made from fire, and then to show Adam was made from clay.
Adding shadda emphasizes the strength of the fire. This fits the context where Iblis (Satan) is boasting about being created from fire.
The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this word follows مِن (from). In this ayah, Iblis is saying Allah created him from fire.

Added وَ (and) at the start to connect this to the previous statement. In the ayah, this connects to خَلَقْتَنِى (You created me), showing Iblis comparing how Allah created him versus Adam.
